mn26:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn26:10.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, would you call subject to sorrow?
mn26:10.2: Wife and children, *bhikkhus*, are subject to sorrow; female and male slaves are subject to sorrow; goats and sheep are subject to sorrow; chickens and pigs are subject to sorrow; elephants, cattle, horses, and mares are subject to sorrow.
mn26:10.3: These acquisitions are subject to sorrow, *bhikkhus*.
mn26:10.4: Yet here one is tied, infatuated, and deeply involved, and being himself subject to sorrow, seeks what is likewise subject to sorrow.
mn26:11.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, would you call subject to defilement?
mn26:11.2: Wife and children, *bhikkhus*, are subject to defilement; female and male slaves are subject to defilement; goats and sheep are subject to defilement; chickens and pigs are subject to defilement; elephants, cattle, horses, and mares are subject to defilement; gold and silver are subject to defilement.
mn26:11.3: These acquisitions are subject to defilement, *bhikkhus*.
mn26:11.4: Yet here one is tied, infatuated, and deeply involved, and being himself subject to defilement, seeks what is likewise subject to defilement.
mn26:12.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the noble search?
mn26:12.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, someone, being himself subject to birth, having understood the drawback in what is subject to birth, seeks the unborn, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being himself subject to aging, having understood the drawback in what is subject to aging, seeks the ageless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being himself subject to illness, having understood the drawback in what is subject to illness, seeks the unafflicted, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being himself subject to death, having understood the drawback in what is subject to death, seeks the deathless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being himself subject to sorrow, having understood the drawback in what is subject to sorrow, seeks the sorrowless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being himself subject to defilement, having understood the drawback in what is subject to defilement, seeks the undefiled, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*.
mn26:13.1: Before my awakening, *bhikkhus*, when I was still unawakened, being only a Bodhisatta, I too, being myself subject to birth, sought what was likewise subject to birth; being myself subject to aging, sought what was likewise subject to aging; being myself subject to illness, sought what was likewise subject to illness; being myself subject to death, sought what was likewise subject to death; being myself subject to sorrow, sought what was likewise subject to sorrow; being myself subject to defilement, sought what was likewise subject to defilement.
mn26:13.3: ‘Why indeed, being myself subject to birth, do I seek what is likewise subject to birth, being myself subject to aging … subject to illness … subject to death … subject to sorrow … being myself subject to defilement, do I seek what is likewise subject to defilement?
mn26:13.4: Suppose that, being myself subject to birth, having understood the drawback in what is subject to birth, I were to seek the unborn, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being myself subject to aging, having understood the drawback in what is subject to aging, I were to seek the ageless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being myself subject to illness, having understood the drawback in what is subject to illness, I were to seek the unafflicted, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being myself subject to death, having understood the drawback in what is subject to death, I were to seek the deathless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being myself subject to sorrow, having understood the drawback in what is subject to sorrow, I were to seek the sorrowless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being myself subject to defilement, having understood the drawback in what is subject to defilement, I were to seek the undefiled, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*.’
mn26:14.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, at a later time, while still young, with black hair, endowed with the blessing of youth in the prime of life, though my mother and father wished otherwise and were weeping with tearful faces, I shaved off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and went forth from the household life into homelessness.
mn26:15.1: Having thus gone forth, while seeking what is skillful, seeking the unsurpassed state of sublime peace, I approached Āḷāra Kālāma. Having approached him, I said to him:
mn26:15.10: Surely Āḷāra Kālāma dwells knowing and seeing this *dhamma*.’
mn26:15.11: Then, *bhikkhus*, I approached Āḷāra Kālāma. Having approached him, I said to him:
mn26:15.12: ‘To what extent, friend Kālāma, do you declare: “Having realized this *dhamma* for myself with direct knowledge, I enter and dwell in it”?’
mn26:15.13: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, Āḷāra Kālāma declared the dimension [*āyatanaṁ*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*].
mn26:15.15: ‘It is not only Āḷāra Kālāma who has faith; I too have faith.
mn26:15.16: It is not only Āḷāra Kālāma who has energy; I too have energy.
mn26:15.17: It is not only Āḷāra Kālāma who has *sati*; I too have *sati*.
mn26:15.18: It is not only Āḷāra Kālāma who has *samādhi*; I too have *samādhi*.
mn26:15.19: It is not only Āḷāra Kālāma who has discernment [*paññā*]; I too have discernment [*paññā*].
mn26:15.2: ‘Friend Kālāma, I wish to live the holy life in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*].’
mn26:15.20: Suppose I were to strive for the realization of that *dhamma* which Āḷāra Kālāma declares he enters and dwells in, having realized it for himself with direct knowledge.’
mn26:15.21: Then, *bhikkhus*, it was not long before I quickly entered and dwelt in that *dhamma*, having realized it for myself with direct knowledge.
mn26:15.22: Then, *bhikkhus*, I approached Āḷāra Kālāma. Having approached him, I said to him:
mn26:15.23: ‘Is it to this extent, friend Kālāma, that you declare: “Having realized this *dhamma* for myself with direct knowledge, I enter and dwell in it”?’
mn26:15.24: ‘It is to this extent, friend, that I declare: “Having realized this *dhamma* for myself with direct knowledge, I enter and dwell in it.”’
mn26:15.25: ‘Friend, I too enter and dwell in this *dhamma* to this extent, having realized it for myself with direct knowledge.’
mn26:15.26: ‘It is a gain for us, friend, well gained indeed for us, friend,
mn26:15.27: that we see the venerable one as such a companion in the holy life.
mn26:15.28: Thus, the *dhamma* that I declare, having realized it for myself with direct knowledge and entered upon it, that same *dhamma* you enter and dwell in, having realized it for yourself with direct knowledge.
mn26:15.29: And the *dhamma* that you enter and dwell in, having realized it for yourself with direct knowledge, that same *dhamma* I declare, having realized it for myself with direct knowledge and entered upon it.
mn26:15.3: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, Āḷāra Kālāma said this to me:
mn26:15.30: Thus, the *dhamma* that I know, that *dhamma* you know; and the *dhamma* that you know, that *dhamma* I know.
mn26:15.31: Thus, as I am, so are you; and as you are, so am I.
mn26:15.32: Come now, friend, let us both look after this group together.’
mn26:15.33: Thus, *bhikkhus*, although Āḷāra Kālāma was my teacher, he placed me, his own pupil, on the same level as himself, and honored me with the highest honor.
mn26:15.35: ‘This *dhamma* does not lead to disillusionment, to dispassion, to cessation, to stilling, to direct knowledge, to awakening, to *nibbāna*, but only to rebirth in the dimension [*āyatanaṁ*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*].’
mn26:15.36: Then, *bhikkhus*, not taking that *dhamma* as enough, becoming disenchanted with that *dhamma*, I departed.
mn26:15.5: This *dhamma* is such that a wise person may soon enter and dwell in it, having realized his own teacher’s doctrine for himself with direct knowledge.’
mn26:15.6: Then, *bhikkhus*, it was not long before I quickly learned that *dhamma*.
mn26:15.7: Then, *bhikkhus*, by mere lip-service, by mere repetition of words, I was speaking words of knowledge and speaking like an elder, and I claimed, ‘I know, I see,’ as did others.
mn26:15.9: ‘Āḷāra Kālāma does not declare, “Having realized this *dhamma* for myself with direct knowledge, I enter and dwell in it,” merely out of faith alone.
mn26:16.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, while still seeking what is skillful, seeking the unsurpassed state of sublime peace, I approached Udaka Rāmaputta. Having approached him, I said to him:
mn26:16.10: Surely Rāma dwelt knowing and seeing this *dhamma*.’
mn26:16.11: Then, *bhikkhus*, I approached Udaka Rāmaputta. Having approached him, I said to him:
mn26:16.12: ‘To what extent, friend, did Rāma declare: “Having realized this *dhamma* for himself with direct knowledge, he enters and dwells in it”?’
mn26:16.13: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, Udaka Rāmaputta declared the dimension [*āyatanaṁ*] of neither-perception-nor-non-perception [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*].
mn26:16.15: ‘It was not only Rāma who had faith; I too have faith.
mn26:16.16: It was not only Rāma who had energy; I too have energy.
mn26:16.17: It was not only Rāma who had *sati*; I too have *sati*.
mn26:16.18: It was not only Rāma who had *samādhi*; I too have *samādhi*.
mn26:16.19: It was not only Rāma who had discernment [*paññā*]; I too have discernment [*paññā*].
mn26:16.2: ‘Friend, I wish to live the holy life in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*].’
mn26:16.20: Suppose I were to strive for the realization of that *dhamma* which Rāma declared he entered and dwelt in, having realized it for himself with direct knowledge.’
mn26:16.21: Then, *bhikkhus*, it was not long before I quickly entered and dwelt in that *dhamma*, having realized it for myself with direct knowledge.
mn26:16.22: Then, *bhikkhus*, I approached Udaka Rāmaputta. Having approached him, I said to him:
mn26:16.23: ‘Is it to this extent, friend, that Rāma declared: “Having realized this *dhamma* for himself with direct knowledge, he entered and dwelt in it”?’
mn26:16.24: ‘It is to this extent, friend, that Rāma declared: “Having realized this *dhamma* for himself with direct knowledge, he entered and dwelt in it.”’
mn26:16.25: ‘Friend, I too enter and dwell in this *dhamma* to this extent, having realized it for myself with direct knowledge.’
mn26:16.26: ‘It is a gain for us, friend, well gained indeed for us, friend,
mn26:16.27: that we see the venerable one as such a companion in the holy life.
mn26:16.28: Thus, the *dhamma* that Rāma declared, having realized it for himself with direct knowledge and entered upon it, that same *dhamma* you enter and dwell in, having realized it for yourself with direct knowledge.
mn26:16.29: And the *dhamma* that you enter and dwell in, having realized it for yourself with direct knowledge, that same *dhamma* Rāma declared, having realized it for himself with direct knowledge and entered upon it.
mn26:16.3: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, Udaka Rāmaputta said this to me:
mn26:16.30: Thus, the *dhamma* that Rāma directly knew, that *dhamma* you know; and the *dhamma* that you know, that *dhamma* Rāma directly knew.
mn26:16.31: Thus, as Rāma was, so are you; and as you are, so was Rāma.
mn26:16.32: Come now, friend, you alone should look after this group.’
mn26:16.33: Thus, *bhikkhus*, Udaka Rāmaputta, though only my fellow companion in the holy life, placed me in the position of teacher and honored me with the highest honor.
mn26:16.35: ‘This *dhamma* does not lead to disillusionment, to dispassion, to cessation, to stilling, to direct knowledge, to awakening, to *nibbāna*, but only to rebirth in the dimension [*āyatanaṁ*] of neither-perception-nor-non-perception [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*].’
mn26:16.36: Then, *bhikkhus*, not taking that *dhamma* as enough, becoming disenchanted with that *dhamma*, I departed.
mn26:16.5: This *dhamma* is such that a wise person may soon enter and dwell in it, having realized his own teacher’s doctrine for himself with direct knowledge.’
mn26:16.6: Then, *bhikkhus*, it was not long before I quickly learned that *dhamma*.
mn26:16.7: Then, *bhikkhus*, by mere lip-service, by mere repetition of words, I was speaking words of knowledge and speaking like an elder, and I claimed, ‘I know, I see,’ as did others.
mn26:16.9: ‘Rāma does not declare, “Having realized this *dhamma* for myself with direct knowledge, I enter and dwell in it,” merely out of faith alone.
mn26:17.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, while still seeking what is skillful, seeking the unsurpassed state of sublime peace, wandering gradually on tour through the Magadhan country, I arrived at Uruvelā, the market town of Senāni.
mn26:17.2: There I saw a delightful stretch of ground, an inspiring woodland grove, and a river flowing with clear water, with lovely smooth banks, and a nearby village suitable for alms.
mn26:17.4: ‘Indeed, this stretch of ground is delightful, and the woodland grove inspiring, and the river flows with clear water, with lovely smooth banks, and a nearby village suitable for alms.
mn26:17.5: This is enough indeed for the striving of one from a good family intent on striving.’
mn26:17.6: Then, *bhikkhus*, I sat down right there—
mn26:18.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, being myself subject to birth, having understood the drawback in what is subject to birth, while seeking the unborn, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*, I attained the unborn, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being myself subject to aging, having understood the drawback in what is subject to aging, while seeking the ageless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*, I attained the ageless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being myself subject to illness, having understood the drawback in what is subject to illness, while seeking the unafflicted, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*, I attained the unafflicted, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being myself subject to death, having understood the drawback in what is subject to death, I attained the deathless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being myself subject to sorrow, having understood the drawback in what is subject to sorrow, I attained the sorrowless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being myself subject to defilement, having understood the drawback in what is subject to defilement, while seeking the undefiled, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*, I attained the undefiled, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*.
mn26:18.2: And knowledge and vision arose in me:
mn26:18.3: ‘Unshakable is my liberation; this is my last birth; now there is no further *bhava*.’
mn26:19.11: by those overcome by passion and hatred.
mn26:19.14: Those dyed with lust will not see it,
mn26:19.15: covered over by a mass of darkness.’
mn26:19.16: Thus, as I reflected, *bhikkhus*, my mind inclined to remaining inactive, not to teaching the *dhamma*.
mn26:19.2: ‘This *dhamma* that has been attained by me is deep, hard to see, hard to understand, peaceful, sublime, beyond the sphere of reasoning, subtle, to be known by the wise.
mn26:19.3: But this generation delights in attachment, relishes attachment, rejoices in attachment.
mn26:19.4: For a generation that delights in attachment, relishes attachment, rejoices in attachment, this state is hard to see, namely this conditionality, *paṭiccasamuppāda*.
mn26:19.5: This state too is hard to see, namely the stilling of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishment of all acquisitions, the ending of *taṇhā*, dispassion, cessation, *nibbāna*.
mn26:19.6: And if I were to teach the *dhamma*, but others would not understand me, that would be wearisome for me, that would be troublesome for me.’
mn26:19.7: Then, *bhikkhus*, these amazing verses, unheard before, came to me:
mn26:19.9: enough now of making it known;
mn26:2.1: Then, in the morning, the *Bhagavā* dressed, took his bowl and robe, and entered Sāvatthī for alms.
mn26:2.2: Then several *bhikkhus* went to Venerable Ānanda. Having gone to Venerable Ānanda, they said this to him:
mn26:2.3: “It has been a long time, friend Ānanda, since we heard a talk on *dhamma* [*dhammī kathā*] in the presence of the *Bhagavā*.
mn26:2.4: It would be good, friend Ānanda, if we might obtain a hearing of a talk on *dhamma* [*dhammiṁ kathaṁ*] in the presence of the *Bhagavā*.”
mn26:2.5: “Then, venerables, go to the hermitage of the brahmin Rammaka.
mn26:2.6: Perhaps you may obtain a hearing of a talk on *dhamma* [*dhammiṁ kathaṁ*] in the presence of the *Bhagavā*.”
mn26:2.7: “Yes, friend,” those *bhikkhus* replied to Venerable Ānanda.
mn26:20.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, Brahmā Sahampati, having known with his own mind the thought in my mind, thought:
mn26:20.11: an impure *dhamma*, conceived by the stained.
mn26:20.12: Open this door to the deathless;
mn26:20.13: let them hear the *dhamma* realized by the stainless one.
mn26:20.14: As one standing on a rocky mountain peak
mn26:20.15: might see the people all around,
mn26:20.17: having ascended the palace built of *dhamma*,
mn26:20.18: look upon the grieving people, free from sorrow,
mn26:20.2: ‘Alas, the world is lost, alas, the world is destroyed, since the mind of the Tathāgata, the *arahant*, the perfectly self-awakened one, inclines to remaining inactive, not to teaching the *dhamma*.’
mn26:20.20: Rise up, hero, victor in battle!
mn26:20.21: Caravan leader, debtless one, wander in the world.
mn26:20.23: there will be those who understand.’
mn26:20.3: Then, *bhikkhus*, just as a strong man might extend his bent arm or bend his extended arm, so too Brahmā Sahampati vanished from the Brahmā world and appeared before me.
mn26:20.4: Then, *bhikkhus*, Brahmā Sahampati, arranging his upper robe over one shoulder, raising his hands in reverence toward me, said this to me:
mn26:20.5: ‘Let the *Bhagavā* teach the *dhamma*, *bhante*; let the Fortunate One teach the *dhamma*.
mn26:20.6: There are beings with little dust in their eyes who are declining because they do not hear the *dhamma*.
mn26:20.7: There will be those who will understand the *dhamma*.’
mn26:20.8: This is what Brahmā Sahampati said, *bhikkhus*.
mn26:20.9: Having said this, he further said:
mn26:21.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, understanding Brahmā’s request, and out of compassion for beings, I surveyed the world with the Buddha-eye.
mn26:21.10: Then, *bhikkhus*, Brahmā Sahampati, thinking, “The *Bhagavā* has granted me an opening for the teaching of the *dhamma*,” bowed to me, circled me keeping his right side toward me, and vanished right there.
mn26:21.2: Surveying the world with the Buddha-eye, I saw beings with little dust in their eyes and with much dust in their eyes, with sharp *indriyas* and dull *indriyas*, with good qualities and bad qualities, easy to instruct and hard to instruct, some dwelling seeing fear in blame and in the next world, and some dwelling not seeing fear in blame and in the next world.
mn26:21.3: Just as in a pond of blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses, some blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses, born in the water and grown in the water, remain submerged beneath the water and thrive while submerged; some blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses, born in the water and grown in the water, stand level with the water; and some blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses, born in the water and grown in the water, rise up out of the water and stand unstained by the water;
mn26:21.4: in the same way, *bhikkhus*, surveying the world with the Buddha-eye, I saw beings with little dust in their eyes and with much dust in their eyes, with sharp *indriyas* and dull *indriyas*, with good qualities and bad qualities, easy to instruct and hard to instruct, some dwelling seeing fear in blame and in the next world, and some dwelling not seeing fear in blame and in the next world.
mn26:21.5: Then, *bhikkhus*, I replied to Brahmā Sahampati in verse:
mn26:21.6: ‘Opened are the doors to the deathless
mn26:21.7: for those who have ears: let them show faith.
mn26:21.8: Perceiving trouble, Brahmā, I did not speak
mn26:21.9: the refined and sublime *dhamma* among humans.’
mn26:22.10: And knowledge and vision arose in me:
mn26:22.13: ‘Āḷāra Kālāma has suffered a great loss.
mn26:22.14: For if he had heard this *dhamma*, he would have quickly understood.’
mn26:22.2: ‘To whom should I first teach the *dhamma*?
mn26:22.5: ‘This Āḷāra Kālāma is wise, competent, and intelligent, and for a long time has had little dust in his eyes.
mn26:22.6: Suppose I were to first teach the *dhamma* to Āḷāra Kālāma.
mn26:22.8: Then, *bhikkhus*, a devatā approached me and said this:
mn26:22.9: ‘*Bhante*, Āḷāra Kālāma died seven days ago.’
mn26:23.10: And knowledge and vision arose in me:
mn26:23.13: ‘Udaka Rāmaputta has suffered a great loss.
mn26:23.14: For if he had heard this *dhamma*, he would have quickly understood.’
mn26:23.2: ‘To whom should I first teach the *dhamma*?
mn26:23.5: ‘This Udaka Rāmaputta is wise, competent, and intelligent, and for a long time has had little dust in his eyes.
mn26:23.6: Suppose I were to first teach the *dhamma* to Udaka Rāmaputta.
mn26:23.8: Then, *bhikkhus*, a devatā approached me and said this:
mn26:24.10: Then, *bhikkhus*, having stayed at Uruvelā as long as I pleased, I set out on tour toward Bārāṇasī.
mn26:24.2: ‘To whom should I first teach the *dhamma*?
mn26:24.5: ‘The group of five *bhikkhus* were very helpful to me, for they attended on me when I was devoted to striving.
mn26:24.6: Suppose I were to first teach the *dhamma* to the group of five *bhikkhus*.’
mn26:24.8: ‘Where are the group of five *bhikkhus* dwelling now?’
mn26:24.9: Then, *bhikkhus*, with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, I saw the group of five *bhikkhus* dwelling at Bārāṇasī in the Deer Park at Isipatana.
mn26:25.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, Upaka the Ājīvaka saw me while I was traveling on the road between Gayā and the Bodhi tree.
mn26:25.11: and one like me is not found.
mn26:25.12: In the world with its devas,
mn26:25.14: For I am an *arahant* in the world,
mn26:25.18: To set rolling the wheel of *dhamma* [*dhammacakkaṁ*],
mn26:25.19: I am going to the city of the Kāsi people.
mn26:25.21: I will beat the drum of the deathless.’
mn26:25.22: ‘Then, friend, as you claim, you are an *arahant*, an endless victor.’
mn26:25.24: who have attained the destruction of the *āsavas*.
mn26:25.25: Evil *dhammas* have been conquered by me,
mn26:25.26: therefore, Upaka, I am a victor.’
mn26:25.27: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, Upaka the Ājīvaka said, ‘It may be so, friend,’ shook his head, took a side road, and departed.
mn26:25.3: ‘Your faculties are serene indeed, friend, your complexion pure and clear.
mn26:25.4: For whose sake have you gone forth, friend? Or who is your teacher? Or whose *dhamma* do you approve of?’
mn26:25.5: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, I addressed Upaka the Ājīvaka in verses:
mn26:25.6: ‘I am one who has overcome all, who knows all,
mn26:25.8: having abandoned all, liberated in the ending of *taṇhā*,
mn26:25.9: having directly known for myself, whom should I point to?
mn26:26.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, traveling gradually on tour, I approached Bārāṇasī, the Deer Park at Isipatana, where the group of five *bhikkhus* were.
mn26:26.2: Then, *bhikkhus*, the group of five *bhikkhus* saw me coming in the distance.
mn26:26.3: Seeing me, they made an agreement among themselves:
mn26:26.4: ‘Here comes that ascetic Gotama, friend, who lives in abundance, who has fallen away from striving, who has turned back to abundance.
mn26:26.5: He should neither be greeted nor risen for, nor should his bowl and robe be received.
mn26:26.6: But a seat may be placed there; if he wishes, he may sit.’
mn26:26.7: But, *bhikkhus*, as I drew nearer and nearer, the group of five *bhikkhus* were unable to keep to their own agreement.
mn26:26.8: Some went to meet me and received my bowl and robe, some prepared a seat, some set out water for the feet.
mn26:26.9: Still, they addressed me by name and as ‘friend.’
mn26:27.1: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, I said this to the group of five *bhikkhus*:
mn26:27.10: The Tathāgata is an *arahant*, perfectly self-awakened.
mn26:27.11: Listen, *bhikkhus*; the deathless has been attained. I instruct; I teach the *dhamma*.
mn26:27.12: Practicing as instructed, you will soon, in this very life, realize for yourselves with direct knowledge and enter upon that unsurpassed culmination of the holy life for the sake of which those from good families rightly go forth from the household life into homelessness.’
mn26:27.13: A second time, *bhikkhus*, the group of five *bhikkhus* said this to me:
mn26:27.14: ‘Even by that conduct, friend Gotama, by that practice, by that difficult undertaking, you did not attain any higher human state, any distinction in noble knowledge and vision. So how could you now, living in abundance, having fallen away from striving, having turned back to abundance, attain any higher human state, any distinction in noble knowledge and vision?’
mn26:27.15: A second time, *bhikkhus*, I said this to the group of five *bhikkhus*:
mn26:27.16: ‘The Tathāgata is not one who lives in abundance …
mn26:27.17: realize for yourselves with direct knowledge and enter upon it in this very life.’
mn26:27.18: A third time, *bhikkhus*, the group of five *bhikkhus* said this to me:
mn26:27.19: ‘Even by that conduct, friend Gotama, by that practice, by that difficult undertaking, you did not attain any higher human state, any distinction in noble knowledge and vision. So how could you now, living in abundance, having fallen away from striving, having turned back to abundance, attain any higher human state, any distinction in noble knowledge and vision?’
mn26:27.2: ‘Do not address the Tathāgata by name or as “friend,” *bhikkhus*.
mn26:27.3: The Tathāgata is an *arahant*, perfectly self-awakened.
mn26:27.4: Listen, *bhikkhus*; the deathless has been attained. I instruct; I teach the *dhamma*.
mn26:27.5: Practicing as instructed, you will soon, in this very life, realize for yourselves with direct knowledge and enter upon that unsurpassed culmination of the holy life for the sake of which those from good families rightly go forth from the household life into homelessness.’
mn26:27.6: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, the group of five *bhikkhus* said this to me:
mn26:27.7: ‘Even by that conduct, friend Gotama, by that practice, by that difficult undertaking, you did not attain any higher human state, any distinction in noble knowledge and vision. So how could you now, living in abundance, having fallen away from striving, having turned back to abundance, attain any higher human state, any distinction in noble knowledge and vision?’
mn26:27.8: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, I said this to the group of five *bhikkhus*:
mn26:27.9: ‘The Tathāgata is not one who lives in abundance, *bhikkhus*; he has not fallen away from striving; he has not turned back to abundance.
mn26:28.1: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, I said this to the group of five *bhikkhus*:
mn26:28.2: ‘Do you remember me ever before making such a declaration as this, *bhikkhus*?’
mn26:28.5: Listen, *bhikkhus*; the deathless has been attained. I instruct; I teach the *dhamma*.
mn26:28.6: Practicing as instructed, you will soon, in this very life, realize for yourselves with direct knowledge and enter upon that unsurpassed culmination of the holy life for the sake of which those from good families rightly go forth from the household life into homelessness.’
mn26:29.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, I was able to persuade the group of five *bhikkhus*.
mn26:29.2: Sometimes, *bhikkhus*, I instructed two *bhikkhus*, while three *bhikkhus* went for alms.
mn26:29.3: With what the three *bhikkhus* brought back from the almsround, the six of us sustained ourselves.
mn26:29.4: Sometimes, *bhikkhus*, I instructed three *bhikkhus*, while two *bhikkhus* went for alms.
mn26:29.5: With what the two *bhikkhus* brought back from the almsround, the six of us sustained ourselves.
mn26:3.1: Then, after the *Bhagavā* had wandered for alms in Sāvatthī and had returned from the almsround after the meal, he addressed Venerable Ānanda:
mn26:3.10: Then Venerable Ānanda said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn26:3.11: “*Bhante*, this hermitage of the brahmin Rammaka is not far away.
mn26:3.12: The hermitage of the brahmin Rammaka is delightful, *bhante*;
mn26:3.13: the hermitage of the brahmin Rammaka is inspiring, *bhante*.
mn26:3.14: It would be good, *bhante*, if the *Bhagavā* would go to the hermitage of the brahmin Rammaka out of compassion.”
mn26:3.2: “Come, Ānanda, let us go to the Eastern Monastery, Migāra’s Mother’s Mansion, for the day’s abiding.”
mn26:3.4: Then the *Bhagavā*, together with Venerable Ānanda, went to the Eastern Monastery, Migāra’s Mother’s Mansion, for the day’s abiding.
mn26:3.5: Then in the evening, when the *Bhagavā* had risen from seclusion, he addressed Venerable Ānanda:
mn26:3.6: “Come, Ānanda, let us go to the Eastern Gatehouse to wash the limbs.”
mn26:3.8: Then the *Bhagavā*, together with Venerable Ānanda, went to the Eastern Gatehouse to wash the limbs.
mn26:3.9: At the Eastern Gatehouse, after washing his limbs, he came up out of the water and stood wearing one robe, drying his limbs.
mn26:30.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, the group of five *bhikkhus*, being thus instructed and advised by me, being themselves subject to birth, having understood the drawback in what is subject to birth, while seeking the unborn, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*, attained the unborn, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being themselves subject to aging, having understood the drawback in what is subject to aging, while seeking the ageless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*, attained the ageless, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*; being themselves subject to illness … being themselves subject to death … being themselves subject to sorrow … being themselves subject to defilement, having understood the drawback in what is subject to defilement, while seeking the undefiled, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*, attained the undefiled, the unsurpassed security from bondage, *nibbāna*.
mn26:30.2: And knowledge and vision arose in them:
mn26:30.3: ‘Unshakable is our liberation; this is our last birth; now there is no further *bhava*.’
mn26:31.1: There are these five strands of sensual pleasure, *bhikkhus*.
mn26:31.3: Forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, lovely, pleasing, agreeable, endearing, connected with sensuality, enticing;
mn26:31.7: tangibles cognizable by the body that are desirable, lovely, pleasing, agreeable, endearing, connected with sensuality, enticing.
mn26:31.8: These, *bhikkhus*, are the five strands of sensual pleasure.
mn26:32.1: Whatever ascetics or brahmins there are, *bhikkhus*, who enjoy these five strands of sensual pleasure, caught up in them, infatuated with them, sunk in them, not seeing the drawback, without discernment [*paññā*] of escape, they should be understood thus:
mn26:32.2: ‘They have fallen into ruin, fallen into disaster, and are at the mercy of the Evil One.’
mn26:32.3: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a wild deer, if bound, were to lie down on a heap of snares.
mn26:32.5: ‘It has fallen into ruin, fallen into disaster, and is at the mercy of the hunter.
mn26:32.6: And when the hunter comes, it will not go wherever it wants.’
mn26:32.7: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, whatever ascetics or brahmins there are who enjoy these five strands of sensual pleasure, caught up in them, infatuated with them, sunk in them, not seeing the drawback, without discernment [*paññā*] of escape, they should be understood thus:
mn26:32.8: ‘They have fallen into ruin, fallen into disaster, and are at the mercy of the Evil One.’
mn26:33.1: But whatever ascetics or brahmins there are, *bhikkhus*, who enjoy these five strands of sensual pleasure without being caught up in them, without being infatuated with them, without being sunk in them, seeing the drawback, with discernment [*paññā*] of escape, they should be understood thus:
mn26:33.2: ‘They have not fallen into ruin, not fallen into disaster, and are not at the mercy of the Evil One.’
mn26:33.3: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a wild deer, unbound, were to lie down on a heap of snares.
mn26:33.5: ‘It has not fallen into ruin, not fallen into disaster, and is not at the mercy of the hunter.
mn26:33.6: And when the hunter comes, it will go wherever it wants.’
mn26:33.7: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, whatever ascetics or brahmins there are who enjoy these five strands of sensual pleasure without being caught up in them, without being infatuated with them, without being sunk in them, seeing the drawback, with discernment [*paññā*] of escape, they should be understood thus:
mn26:33.8: ‘They have not fallen into ruin, not fallen into disaster, and are not at the mercy of the Evil One.’
mn26:34.1: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a wild deer wandering freely in the forest wilderness goes at ease, stands at ease, sits at ease, and lies down at ease.
mn26:34.3: Because, *bhikkhus*, it has gone beyond the hunter’s range.
mn26:34.4: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*].
mn26:34.5: This is called, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* who has blinded Māra, become trackless, and gone beyond the sight of the Evil One after destroying Māra’s eye.
mn26:35.1: Furthermore, *bhikkhus*, with the settling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with inward clarity and unification [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] of mind [*citta*], a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*, which is without thought [*avitakkaṁ*] and without examination [*avicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*.
mn26:35.2: This is called, *bhikkhus*, … the Evil One.
mn26:36.1: Furthermore, *bhikkhus*, with the fading away of *pīti*, a *bhikkhu* dwells equanimous [*upekkhako*], with *sati* and fully aware [*sampajāno*], and experiences *sukha* with the body. He enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*, of which the noble ones say: ‘Equanimous, with *sati* [*satimā*], one dwells happily.’
mn26:36.2: This is called, *bhikkhus*, … the Evil One.
mn26:37.1: Furthermore, *bhikkhus*, with the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of *dukkha*, and with the earlier disappearance of joy and grief, a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither painful nor pleasant, with purity of equanimity and *sati* [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*].
mn26:37.2: This is called, *bhikkhus*, … the Evil One.
mn26:38.1: Furthermore, *bhikkhus*, with the complete transcending of perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of resistance, with non-attention to perceptions of diversity, thinking, ‘Space is infinite,’ a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of boundless space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*].
mn26:38.2: This is called, *bhikkhus*, … the Evil One.
mn26:39.1: Furthermore, *bhikkhus*, with the complete transcending of the dimension of boundless space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘Consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] is infinite,’ a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of boundless consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*].
mn26:39.2: This is called, *bhikkhus*, … the Evil One.
mn26:4.1: Then the *Bhagavā* went to the hermitage of the brahmin Rammaka.
mn26:4.11: This is fitting for you, sons of good families who have gone forth from the household life into homelessness out of faith, that you should sit together for a talk on *dhamma* [*dhammiyā kathāya*].
mn26:4.12: When you have gathered together, *bhikkhus*, two things are to be done by you—
mn26:4.13: either a talk on *dhamma* [*dhammī kathā*], or noble silence.
mn26:4.2: Now at that time several *bhikkhus* were sitting in the hermitage of the brahmin Rammaka engaged in a talk on *dhamma* [*dhammiyā kathāya*].
mn26:4.3: Then the *Bhagavā* stood in the entrance hall outside, waiting for the talk to end.
mn26:4.4: Then, knowing that the talk had ended, the *Bhagavā* coughed and knocked at the bolt.
mn26:4.5: Those *bhikkhus* opened the door for the *Bhagavā*.
mn26:4.6: Then the *Bhagavā* entered the hermitage of the brahmin Rammaka and sat down on the seat prepared.
mn26:4.7: Having sat down, the *Bhagavā* addressed the *bhikkhus*:
mn26:4.8: “For what discussion are you sitting together now, *bhikkhus*? And what unfinished conversation was there among you?”
mn26:4.9: “It was concerning the *Bhagavā* himself, *bhante*, that our talk on *dhamma* [*dhammī kathā*] had been left unfinished. Then the *Bhagavā* arrived.”
mn26:40.1: Furthermore, *bhikkhus*, with the complete transcending of the dimension of boundless consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘There is nothing,’ a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*].
mn26:40.2: This is called, *bhikkhus*, … the Evil One.
mn26:41.1: Furthermore, *bhikkhus*, with the complete transcending of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of neither-perception-nor-non-perception [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*].
mn26:41.2: This is called, *bhikkhus*, … the Evil One.
mn26:42.1: Furthermore, *bhikkhus*, with the complete transcending of the dimension of neither-perception-nor-non-perception [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*], a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the cessation of perception and feeling [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*], and having seen with discernment [*paññāya*], his *āsavas* are exhausted.
mn26:42.2: This is called, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* who has blinded Māra, become trackless, and gone beyond the sight of the Evil One after destroying Māra’s eye.
mn26:42.3: Having crossed over the entanglement in the world, he goes at ease, stands at ease, sits at ease, and lies down at ease.
mn26:42.5: Because, *bhikkhus*, he has gone beyond the Evil One’s range.”
mn26:42.7: Delighted, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* said.
mn26:42.8: The Mass of Snares is finished, the sixth.
mn26:5.2: the noble search and the ignoble search.
mn26:5.3: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the ignoble search?
mn26:5.4: Here, *bhikkhus*, someone, being himself subject to birth, seeks what is likewise subject to birth; being himself subject to aging, seeks what is likewise subject to aging; being himself subject to illness, seeks what is likewise subject to illness; being himself subject to death, seeks what is likewise subject to death; being himself subject to sorrow, seeks what is likewise subject to sorrow; being himself subject to defilement, seeks what is likewise subject to defilement.
mn26:6.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, would you call subject to birth?
mn26:6.2: Wife and children, *bhikkhus*, are subject to birth; female and male slaves are subject to birth; goats and sheep are subject to birth; chickens and pigs are subject to birth; elephants, cattle, horses, and mares are subject to birth; gold and silver are subject to birth.
mn26:6.3: These acquisitions are subject to birth, *bhikkhus*.
mn26:6.4: Yet here one is tied, infatuated, and deeply involved, and being himself subject to birth, seeks what is likewise subject to birth.
mn26:7.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, would you call subject to aging?
mn26:7.2: Wife and children, *bhikkhus*, are subject to aging; female and male slaves are subject to aging; goats and sheep are subject to aging; chickens and pigs are subject to aging; elephants, cattle, horses, and mares are subject to aging; gold and silver are subject to aging.
mn26:7.3: These acquisitions are subject to aging, *bhikkhus*.
mn26:7.4: Yet here one is tied, infatuated, and deeply involved, and being himself subject to aging, seeks what is likewise subject to aging.
mn26:8.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, would you call subject to illness?
mn26:8.2: Wife and children, *bhikkhus*, are subject to illness; female and male slaves are subject to illness; goats and sheep are subject to illness; chickens and pigs are subject to illness; elephants, cattle, horses, and mares are subject to illness.
mn26:8.3: These acquisitions are subject to illness, *bhikkhus*.
mn26:8.4: Yet here one is tied, infatuated, and deeply involved, and being himself subject to illness, seeks what is likewise subject to illness.
mn26:9.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, would you call subject to death?
mn26:9.2: Wife and children, *bhikkhus*, are subject to death; female and male slaves are subject to death; goats and sheep are subject to death; chickens and pigs are subject to death; elephants, cattle, horses, and mares are subject to death.
mn26:9.3: These acquisitions are subject to death, *bhikkhus*.
mn26:9.4: Yet here one is tied, infatuated, and deeply involved, and being himself subject to death, seeks what is likewise subject to death.
